hints.nominative: Nominative would also be 'Pater,' but this is direct address — vocative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'patris' ends in -is
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'patris' ends in -is
hints.4th declension: 4th declension has genitive in -ūs — 'patris' ends in -is

steps.reveal: in manus tuas = into your hands (acc. expressing entrusting)

hints.location: 'in your hands': 'In' + ablative would express location — 'in' + accusative expresses direction/entrusting
hints.purpose: 'for your hands': Purpose uses 'ad' or a purpose clause — 'in' + accusative here is directional
hints.accompaniment: 'with your hands': 'Cum' + ablative expresses accompaniment — 'in' + accusative is directional

hints.nominative: Nominative plural is also 'manus,' but 'tuas' shows feminine accusative plural
hints.ablative: Ablative plural would be 'manibus tuis' — 'manus tuas' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive plural would be 'manuum tuarum' — 'manus tuas' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'manus' (gen.) ends in -ūs
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'manus' (gen.) ends in -ūs
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'manus' (gen.) ends in -ūs

hints.to command: 'To command' is 'imperare' or 'iubere' — 'commendare' means to entrust
hints.to recommend: While 'commendare' can mean recommend, the primary sense here is to entrust into safekeeping
hints.to remember: 'To remember' is 'meminisse' or 'commemorare' — 'commendare' means to entrust

hints.perfect indicative: Perfect would be 'commendavi' — 'commendo' is present indicative
hints.future indicative: Future would be 'commendabo' — 'commendo' is present indicative
hints.present subjunctive: Present subjunctive would be 'commendem' — 'commendo' is present indicative

hints.mando, mandare, mandavi, mandatum: These are the parts of 'mandare' (to entrust/command) — similar but different verb
hints.committo, committere, commisi, commissum: These are the parts of 'committere' (to join/entrust) — different compound
hints.commemoro, commemorare, commemoravi, com: These are the parts of 'commemorare' (to recall) — different compound

steps.reveal: spiritum meum = my spirit (acc., object of 'commendo')

hints.nominative: Nominative would be 'spiritus meus' — 'spiritum meum' with -um is accusative
hints.ablative: Ablative would be 'spiritu meo' — 'spiritum meum' is accusative
hints.genitive: Genitive would be 'spiritus mei' — 'spiritum meum' is accusative

hints.1st declension: 1st declension has genitive in -ae — 'spiritus' (gen.) ends in -ūs
hints.2nd declension: 2nd declension has genitive in -i — 'spiritus' (gen.) ends in -ūs
hints.3rd declension: 3rd declension has genitive in -is — 'spiritus' (gen.) ends in -ūs

steps.prompt: This verse quotes from which Old Testament psalm?
steps.target: Psalm 31 (30):6 — 'In manus tuas commendo spiritum meum'
choices: Psalm 31 (30):6 — 'In manus tuas commendo spiritum meum'

hints.Psalm 22 (21):1 — 'Deus meus quare me de: That is the cry from Matthew/Mark's crucifixion — Luke's 'in manus tuas' quotes Psalm 31
hints.Psalm 23 (22):1 — 'Dominus pascit me': That is the shepherd psalm — 'in manus tuas commendo' comes from Psalm 31
hints.Psalm 51 (50):1 — 'Miserere mei Deus': That is David's penitential psalm — 'in manus tuas' is from Psalm 31

steps.prompt: This is Jesus's last word in Luke's Gospel. How does the use of present tense 'commendo' differ from a past tense?
steps.target: the present tense makes the act of entrusting immediate and volitional
choices: the present tense makes the act of entrusting immediate and volitional
the present tense is used because the action is incomplete
the present tense is a historical present for past events
there is no difference in meaning

hints.the present tense is used because the ac: The action is being completed at this moment — the present expresses the immediacy of the act
hints.the present tense is a historical presen: Historical presents narrate past events — but Jesus is speaking in the present moment of his death
hints.there is no difference in meaning: The choice of present tense is significant — it shows active, conscious surrender at the moment of death

translation: Father, into your hands I commend my spirit
